content:
6 min read
Just now

--

A story about the 40 seconds that decide whether anyone pays you, and the embarrassing amount of time I spent ignoring it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Hey! I’m Anukar. If you’ve read my writeups before, you know me as the guy who finds bugs / vulnerabilities.
This story is different. This time the thing that was broken… was mine. And nobody paid me a bounty for finding it XD

The Setup
Earlier this year I did what every developer eventually does: I built a SaaS. Nights, weekends, way too much coffee. Auth, billing, editor, analytics and lot more! I was proud of it the way you’re proud of your first CTF solve.
Launch day comes; I post everywhere… Product Hunt, X, Reddit, the group chats. Traffic actually shows up; about 4,000 visitors in the first week.
Signups? 03
One was my friend and one was me testing on my phone itself, So really: 1 XD
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Launch week analyticsA 0.07% conversion rate. To put that in perspective, you could convert more people by standing on the street and asking strangers to sign up.

The Discovery
My first instinct was very developer-brained: the product must not be good enough yet. So I did what we all do. I built more features…
Three weeks of shipping, conversion rate after: basically identical.
That’s when I stopped and did something I should have done on day one. I watched session recordings of real visitors on my landing page. One by one…
The pattern was so consistent it hurt:
1. Visitor lands on the page
2. Reads the headline (2–3s)
3. Scrolls past the feature list without reading it
4. Hovers over the screenshots for a moment
5. Leaves at around the 40-second mark
Nobody was rejecting my product. They never *experienced* it. They saw a wall of claims and pictures, shrugged, and went back to their tabs.
And here’s the thing that stung: I would have done exactly the same on someone else’s site.

The Technical Breakdown
I went down a rabbit hole of buyer psychology that week, and three concepts explained everything I was seeing in those recordings.
1. Nobody believes claims. Everybody believes experiences :)
There’s a reason IKEA lets you sit on the furniture and car dealers hand you the keys. Psychologists call it the endowment effect; the moment you interact with something, your brain starts treating it as partially yours. Losing it starts to feel like a loss.
A screenshot can’t trigger that. A feature list definitely can’t. Interaction can.
2. The “Spark moment” has a deadline.
Every product has one moment where the visitor goes ”ohh, THAT’S what this does.” In my recordings, I had roughly 40 seconds to cause it. My spark moment was hidden behind a signup wall, an onboarding flow, and an empty dashboard. That’s like a demo with the payoff on step 14 of 14.
3. Showing beats telling, but *doing* beats showing.
Video demos get skipped (they require audio, patience, and surrendering control). Text gets skimmed. But give someone something to click and a weird thing happens: they finish it. Completion becomes a tiny game.
> People don’t buy software. They buy the moment they *get it*. Everything on your landing page either causes that moment faster, or delays it.

What I Actually Changed
So I rebuilt the top of my landing page around one idea: let visitors use the product before they sign up.
Not a video, not screenshots with arrows. but an actual clickable, guided walkthrough of the real interface, embedded right under the headline. Click through the core flow, feel the product, no account needed.
I tried two ways of getting there. Documenting both because the first one cost me a weekend you can skip.
Method 1: build the walkthrough myself
Developer ego said “it’s just a carousel, how hard can it be.” So I hand-rolled one: screenshots of each screen, arrows drawn in Figma, a little Next.js component with click zones and step state.

Get Anukar’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

It worked, technically. But:
- Screenshots blur the moment anyone zooms or opens it on mobile
- Every tiny UI change meant re-shooting and re-annotating everything (my UI changes weekly)
- It looked like a slideshow of my product, not my product; visitors could tell
- The weekend I spent building it was a weekend not spent on the actual product
Verdict: fine for a one-off pitch deck, terrible as a living landing-page asset.
Method 2: use a demo tool
So I went shopping for interactive demo software, and immediately hit a wall: the established tools are priced for enterprises. I researched this properly — $350–$575/month entry tiers, annual contracts, and some of them make you book a sales call just to see pricing. For a bootstrapped solo dev, that’s not a tool, that’s a second rent.
Then I found Mirage (usemirage.io), which is basically the same idea priced for people like us. What made it stick for me as a solo founder:
- Free first demo, full editor, no card; I was testing it on my real app within minutes
- It captures the actual DOM of your app, not screenshots; text stays sharp, layouts stay responsive, and the demo feels like the product (the security-researcher in me refuses to call image slideshows “demos” XD)
- ~90 seconds from capture to a live, embeddable demo; UI changed? Re-capture, republish, every embed updates
- Flat $20/mo for unlimited demos; no per-seat math, no sales call
- Lead capture + per-step drop-off analytics built in, which matters in a minute
I captured my core flow with their Chrome extension, added guided hotspots, and embedded it under the headline the same afternoon my hand-rolled carousel went in the bin.

What Came Back
Press enter or click to view image in full size

After using usemirage.ioSame traffic sources, same product underneath, but New first 40 seconds!
- Time on page: roughly doubled; people who started the demo stuck around to finish it
- Signup conversion 0.07%: a bit over 2% in the first month (about 28×)
- Quality: this one surprised me most. People arrived in the app already knowing what to do, because the demo *was* the onboarding
And the wild part: the demo itself became content. People shared the link around (try this, it’s clickable) in a way nobody ever shares a pricing page.

Key Takeaways
For founders and indie hackers:
1. Watch recordings before writing code. One hour of session replays taught me more than three weeks of feature work.
2. Find your 40-second spark moment. If a stranger can’t experience your core value inside a minute, your landing page has a bug. Treat it like one.
3. Interaction > video > screenshots > text. In that order. Every level down, trust drops.
4. Your demo is your best salesperson. It works at 3am, never misquotes the product, and scales to every visitor at once.
5. Instrument the demo. Step-level drop-off shows the exact screen where interest dies. Fix that screen. Repeat.

Final Thoughts
The uncomfortable lesson: I spent months making the product better and zero days making it believable. Nobody was ever going to pay for something they hadn’t felt.
If you’re sitting on a good product with a dead landing page, don’t build another feature this weekend. Watch ten session recordings, find your spark moment, and put it in front of the signup wall.
And learn from my wasted weekend: don’t hand-roll the walkthrough. The tool I ended up using is free to try: usemirage.io. Capture your app, get a clickable demo embedded in about 90 seconds and the first demo’s free, no card, no sales call (obviously; you just read 1,500 words about why I hate those) haha!
Thanks for reading till here!
